content: I'll start by reading my role and task context.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.LineBundlePullback

9	/-!
10	# Coherence of locally trivial line bundles (A.2.c engine)
11	
12	This file is the **iter-256 Lane engine** file-skeleton for the cheap, on-path
13	half of the line-bundle coherence requirement of the A.2.c Quot-scheme
14	embedding (`chap:Picard_QuotScheme`, `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`).
15	
16	The Quot functor takes coherent quotients of a coherent sheaf; to feed it a line
17	bundle one must know that the line bundle is itself coherent, i.e. a finitely
18	presented `𝒪`-module. The embedding's input is a point of the relative Picard
19	scheme `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, whose carrier is the **locally trivial** predicate
20	`IsLocallyTrivial` of `chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback` (the project-side stand-in
21	for an invertible sheaf). The chapter proves
22	`IsLocallyTrivial M ⟹ M.IsFinitePresentation`, together with a rank-one /
23	flatness record. The general implication "`M` tensor-invertible `⟹` `M` locally
24	free of rank one" (the converse spreading-out, Stacks Lemma 0B8M when stalks are
25	local) is *not* crossed here: the carrier is already locally trivial, so the
26	finite-presentation spreading-out is never needed.
27	
28	## Status (iter-258: 1 sorry → 0 locally; redirected to shared root)
29	
30	The engine file is now **locally `sorry`-free**. The last remaining ingredient,
31	the over↔restrict bridge `chartOverIso`, has been redirected to the general
32	`Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`
33	(the iter-258 shared-root pivot). That general bridge is a complete one-line
34	composite; its remaining mathematical content (the three isos `overEquivalence`,
35	`restrictOverIso`, `unitOverIso`) lives in the shared-root file, where the dual
36	lane (`DualInverse.lean`) consumes the same construction. Once those three close,
37	this engine becomes fully axiom-clean with no further edits here.
38	
39	Of the five pinned declarations, four were closed axiom-clean and the fifth
40	(`chartPresentation`) is complete modulo the (now redirected) `chartOverIso`:
41	
42	* `exists_trivializing_cover` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): repackage the pointwise
43	  `IsLocallyTrivial` existential as an indexed affine cover.
44	* `chart_free_rank_one` — CLOSED (axiom-clean): the chart-local trivialisation.
45	* `chartPresentation` — built as `unitPresentation.ofIsIso chartOverIso.inv`; its
46	  `IsFinite` rides on the `ofIsIso` instance. Reduces to `chartOverIso`.

58	trivialisation bridge: an isomorphism `M.over U ≅ unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`
59	transporting the given scheme-level trivialisation
60	`e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ unit (↑U).ringCatSheaf`. The two source categories are
61	equivalent via the open-immersion site equivalence
62	`Opens.overEquivalence U : Over U ≌ Opens ↥U` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Over`);
63	lifting that equivalence to the level of *sheaves of modules* is the shared root
64	`Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence` of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`,
65	and the bridge is the general `Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` built there. This file

72	   repackage the pointwise existential of `IsLocallyTrivial` into an indexed
73	   trivialising affine cover.

82	   rank-one free record.

84	## Site instances (the chief de-risk; see `rem:lbc_site_instances`)
85	
86	Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation` / `QuasicoherentData` are stated
87	under three site hypotheses on the slice topologies `J.over U` of the underlying
88	topology `J = Opens.grothendieckTopology X` of `X.ringCatSheaf`:
89	
90	```
91	[∀ U, (J.over U).HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat AddCommGrpCat)]
92	[∀ U, HasWeakSheafify (J.over U) AddCommGrpCat]     -- (resp. HasSheafify)
93	[∀ U, (J.over U).WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat]
94	```
95	
96	The de-risk outcome (whether these resolve for `X.ringCatSheaf`) is recorded in
97	the task result and probed by the `#check` commands at the end of this file.

100	
101	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_LineBundleCoherence.tex`.
102	Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Definition 17.25.1
103	(Tag 01CS, invertible modules), "Modules of finite presentation", and
104	Lemma 17.25.4 (Tag 0B8M).

107	set_option autoImplicit false
108	
109	universe u
110	
111	open CategoryTheory Limits
112	
113	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
114	
115	namespace Scheme
116	
117	namespace LineBundle
118	
119	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
120	
121	/-! ## §1. From pointwise triviality to a trivialising cover -/
122	
123	/-- **Trivialising affine cover** (Stacks 01CS / Definition 17.25.1).
124	A locally trivial module `M` admits an indexed trivialising affine cover: an
125	index type `I`, a family of affine opens `U i` covering `X`, and for each `i` a
126	`𝒪_{U_i}`-module isomorphism `M|_{U_i} ≅ 𝒪_{U_i}`.
127	
128	The witness repackages the pointwise existential `IsLocallyTrivial M` (take
129	`I := X` and assign each point its chosen neighbourhood). -/
130	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.exists_trivializing_cover {M : X.Modules}
131	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
132	    ∃ (I : Type u) (U : I → X.Opens),
133	      (∀ i, IsAffineOpen (U i)) ∧ iSup U = ⊤ ∧
134	        ∀ i, Nonempty (M.restrict (U i).ι ≅
135	          SheafOfModules.unit (U i : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
136	  refine ⟨X, fun x => (hM x).choose, fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.1, ?_,
137	    fun x => (hM x).choose_spec.2.2⟩
138	  rw [eq_top_iff]
139	  intro x _
140	  rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup]
141	  exact ⟨x, (hM x).choose_spec.1⟩
142	
143	/-! ## §1b. The canonical finite presentation of the unit module -/
144	
145	section UnitPresentation
146	
147	universe u₁ v₁
148	
149	variable {C : Type u₁} [Category.{v₁} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
150	  {R : Sheaf J RingCat.{u}}
151	  [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{u}] [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat.{u}]
152	  [J.HasSheafCompose (forget₂ RingCat.{u} AddCommGrpCat.{u})]
153	
154	/-- The canonical isomorphism `free PUnit ≅ unit R`: the free module on a single
155	generator is the unit module. -/
156	noncomputable def freeUnitIso :
157	    SheafOfModules.free.{u} (R := R) PUnit.{u + 1} ≅ SheafOfModules.unit R :=
158	  Limits.coproductUniqueIso (fun (_ : PUnit.{u + 1}) ↦ SheafOfModules.unit R)
159	
160	/-- The canonical generating sections of `unit R`: one generator (the image of
161	the free generator under `freeUnitIso`). -/
162	noncomputable def unitGenerators : (SheafOfModules.unit R).GeneratingSections where
163	  I := PUnit.{u + 1}
164	  s := (SheafOfModules.unit R).freeHomEquiv freeUnitIso.hom
165	  epi := by
166	    rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
167	    infer_instance
168	
169	/-- The canonical finite presentation of the unit module `unit R`: one generator,
170	no relations (its generating map is an isomorphism, so the relation kernel is the
171	zero object). -/
172	noncomputable def unitPresentation : (SheafOfModules.unit R).Presentation where
173	  generators := unitGenerators
174	  relations :=
175	    { I := PEmpty.{u + 1}
176	      s := PEmpty.elim
177	      epi := by
178	        have hiso : IsIso (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := by
179	          dsimp only [unitGenerators, SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.π]
180	          rw [Equiv.symm_apply_apply]
181	          infer_instance
182	        have : Mono (unitGenerators (R := R)).π := inferInstance
183	        have hz : Limits.IsZero (Limits.kernel (unitGenerators (R := R)).π) :=
184	          Limits.isZero_kernel_of_mono _
185	        exact ⟨fun g h _ => hz.eq_of_src g h⟩ }
186	
187	instance : (unitPresentation (R := R)).IsFinite where
188	  isFiniteType_generators := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PUnit.{u + 1})⟩
189	  isFiniteType_relations := ⟨inferInstanceAs (Finite PEmpty.{u + 1})⟩
190	
191	end UnitPresentation
192	
193	/-! ## §2. The trivial presentation on a chart -/
194	
195	/-- **The over↔restrict trivialisation bridge.** Transports the scheme-level
196	trivialisation `e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U` (an isomorphism in
197	`SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf`, the category of modules on the *open
198	subscheme* `U`) to a slice-level trivialisation
199	`M.over U ≅ 𝒪_U` (in `SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`, the category of
200	modules on the *slice site* `J.over U`).
201	
202	The two source categories are equivalent — the open-immersion functor
203	`U.ι.opensFunctor : Opens ↥U ≌ {V ∈ Opens X | V ≤ U} = Over U` induces an
204	equivalence `SheafOfModules (↑U).ringCatSheaf ≌ SheafOfModules (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)`
205	identifying `M.restrict U.ι ↦ M.over U` and `unit ↦ unit`. That modules-level
206	lift of `Opens.overEquivalence` is the shared root `Scheme.Modules.overEquivalence`
207	of `AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.SheafOverEquivalence`, and the bridge is the general
208	`Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso` built there as the three-step composite
209	`(restrictOverIso).symm ≪≫ overEquivalence.functor.mapIso e ≪≫ unitOverIso`.
210	
211	This local declaration now **redirects** to that general construction (the
212	iter-258 shared-root pivot), so it carries no `sorry` of its own; the remaining
213	mathematical content (the three isos `overEquivalence` / `restrictOverIso` /
214	`unitOverIso`) lives in the shared-root file where the dual lane consumes it too.
215	Everything else in this engine (`chartPresentation`, `isFinitePresentation`,
216	`isFiniteType`) was already complete modulo this isomorphism. -/
217	noncomputable def chartOverIso (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
218	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
219	    M.over U ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U) :=
220	  Scheme.Modules.chartOverIso U M e
221	
222	/-- **Finite free presentation on a chart** (Stacks, "Modules of finite
223	presentation"). On a trivialising chart `U` — i.e. with an isomorphism
224	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` — the restriction `M.over U` admits the trivial finite free
225	presentation (one generator, no relations), obtained by transporting the
226	canonical presentation of `SheafOfModules.unit` along the trivialisation
227	(through the `chartOverIso` bridge). -/
228	noncomputable def IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
229	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
230	    (M.over U).Presentation := by
231	  exact SheafOfModules.Presentation.ofIsIso (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U)
232	    (M := SheafOfModules.unit (X.ringCatSheaf.over U)) (N := SheafOfModules.over M U)
233	    (chartOverIso M U e).inv (unitPresentation (R := X.ringCatSheaf.over U))
234	
235	instance (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens)
236	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
237	    (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M U e).IsFinite := by
238	  dsimp only [IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation]
239	  infer_instance
240	
241	/-! ## §3. Finite presentation of a locally trivial line bundle -/
242	
243	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finitely presented** (Stacks 0B8M / Lemma 17.25.4).
244	A locally trivial module on a scheme is finitely presented: assemble a
245	`QuasicoherentData` from the trivialising affine cover
246	(`exists_trivializing_cover`), whose presentation at each index is the finite
247	free presentation of `chartPresentation`, and feed it to
248	`SheafOfModules.IsFinitePresentation.mk`. No stalk computation or
249	neighbourhood-spreading is involved. -/
250	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFinitePresentation {M : X.Modules}
251	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
252	    M.IsFinitePresentation := by
253	  obtain ⟨I, U, hUaff, hUtop, hUiso⟩ := hM.exists_trivializing_cover
254	  -- assemble the quasi-coherent data, one finite chart presentation per index;
255	  -- `shrink` lands the index type in the site-object universe demanded by
256	  -- `IsFinitePresentation`'s existential.
257	  let q : M.QuasicoherentData :=
258	    { I := I
259	      X := U
260	      coversTop := by
261	        -- the trivialising affine opens cover the terminal object of the site
262	        intro W x hxW
263	        have hx : x ∈ iSup U := by rw [hUtop]; trivial
264	        rw [TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_iSup] at hx
265	        obtain ⟨i, hi⟩ := hx
266	        exact ⟨U i ⊓ W, homOfLE inf_le_right, ⟨i, ⟨homOfLE inf_le_left⟩⟩, hi, hxW⟩
267	      presentation := fun i => IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M (U i) (hUiso i).some }
268	  have hsh : q.shrink.IsFinitePresentation := by
269	    apply SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData.IsFinitePresentation.mk
270	    intro i
271	    exact inferInstanceAs (IsLocallyTrivial.chartPresentation M _ _).IsFinite
272	  exact { exists_quasicoherentData := ⟨q.shrink, hsh⟩ }
273	
274	/-- **Locally trivial `⟹` finite type and quasi-coherent** (corollary of
275	`isFinitePresentation`). Finite presentation implies finite type by Mathlib's
276	`SheafOfModules.instIsFiniteTypeOfIsFinitePresentation` and quasi-coherence by
277	`SheafOfModules.instIsQuasicoherentOfIsFinitePresentation`. -/
278	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.isFiniteType {M : X.Modules}
279	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) :
280	    M.IsFiniteType := by
281	  haveI := hM.isFinitePresentation
282	  infer_instance
283	
284	/-! ## §4. Rank one and flatness record -/
285	
286	/-- **Chart-local rank one and flatness** (Stacks, "Locally free sheaves":
287	finite locally free of rank `r`). On a trivialising chart the restriction
288	`M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U` is free of rank one over `𝒪_U`; in particular flat (`𝒪_U` is a flat
289	module over itself, and flatness is preserved by the isomorphism). The fact is
290	recorded chart-locally — as the rank-one free model `M|_U ≅ 𝒪_U = ⊕_{*} 𝒪_U`
291	the Quot embedding consumes — because Mathlib has no `SheafOfModules`-level
292	locally-free / flat / rank predicate. -/
293	theorem IsLocallyTrivial.chart_free_rank_one {M : X.Modules}
294	    (hM : IsLocallyTrivial M) (x : X) :
295	    ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
296	      Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) := by
297	  exact hM x
298	
299	end LineBundle
300	
301	end Scheme
302	
303	end AlgebraicGeometry

content: I'll start by reading the key context files to understand my task.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).
133

1	# Analogy: `pushforwardComp_lax_μ` — monoidality of `PresheafOfModules.pushforward` across composition
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	ana-pclm260
8	
9	## Iteration
10	260
11	
12	## Question
13	For the lone D3′ residual `pushforwardComp_lax_μ` (the lax tensorator `μ` of `pushforward ψ ⋙ pushforward φ`
14	equals the `μ` of the single `pushforward (φ ≫ F.op ◁ ψ)`): does Mathlib already have this coherence;
15	what is the canonical PROOF SHAPE; is `pushforwardComp = Iso.refl` enough to make it reduce to
16	`μ_natural`/`map_comp`; and if not, what is the most economical project-local construction?
17	
18	## Project artifact(s)
19	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:2143` — `pushforwardComp_lax_μ` (the open `sorry`).
20	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:1124` — `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` (the RHS's LaxMonoidal instance; `exact h` of `(pushforward₀OfCommRingCat F R₀ ⋙ restrictScalars φ').LaxMonoidal`).
21	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/PresheafInternalHom.lean:306,323` — `restrictScalarsLaxμ` / `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` (presheaf `restrictScalars` μ, **defined sectionwise** as the ModuleCat μ).
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean:1674` — `epsilonPresheafToSheafUnit` (the ε-twin; the working sectionwise template).
23	
24	## Decisions identified
25	
26	### Decision 1: Does Mathlib have "pushforward is (lax) monoidal pseudofunctorially"?
27	
28	- **Mathlib idiom**: NONE. Mathlib's `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Pushforward.lean`
29	  has **zero monoidal content** — `pushforward` is just `pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ` (line 86),
30	  and `pushforwardComp φ ψ := Iso.refl` (line 135) is only the **underlying-functor** iso
31	  (`pushforward ψ ⋙ pushforward φ ≅ pushforward (φ ≫ whiskerLeft F.op ψ)`), with `pushforward_assoc`
32	  / `pushforward_comp_id` / `pushforward_id_comp` (lines 142–157) the pseudofunctor coherences — all
33	  about the *functors*, none about a *lax tensorator*. The lax-monoidal structure on
34	  `PresheafOfModules.pushforward` is **entirely project-built** (`presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal`,
35	  resting on the project's `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`). So there is no Mathlib lemma "pushforward is
36	  monoidal across composition" to cite, and none of `restrictScalarsComp` / `extendScalarsComp` /
37	  `homEquiv_extendScalarsComp` proves it (those concern the change-of-rings *functors'* associativity,
38	  not the lax tensorator μ).
39	- **Project's path**: build `pushforwardComp_lax_μ` as a project-local supplement.
40	- **Gap**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL — but a *small* one (the workhorse, `restrictScalars_μ_tmul`, is in Mathlib).
41	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL.
42	
43	### Decision 2: Is the proof a ~150-LOC `extendScalarsComp` change-of-rings build, or a short sectionwise reduction?
44	
45	- **Mathlib idiom (the workhorse)**: `ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul`
46	  (`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean:116`):
47	  `μ (restrictScalars f) M₁ M₂ (m₁ ⊗ₜ m₂) = m₁ ⊗ₜ m₂`. The lax tensorator of `restrictScalars`
48	  is the **identity on pure tensors**. Combined with `pushforward₀OfCommRingCat F R`.Monoidal having
49	  `μIso := Iso.refl` (`Mathlib/.../Presheaf/PushforwardZeroMonoidal.lean:33`, so its μ is `𝟙`), and
50	  `Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp` being `@[simps]` (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Functor.lean:221`,
51	  giving `comp_μ : μ (F⋙G) X Y = μ G _ _ ≫ G.map (μ F X Y)`), **both μ's collapse, sectionwise on a
52	  pure tensor, to `m ⊗ₜ n`.** So the proof is a SHORT sectionwise tensor-induction, NOT a 150-LOC
53	  `extendScalarsComp`/`homEquiv_extendScalarsComp` build. The docstring's feared route (and the
54	  directive's Q1/Q2 candidates `extendScalarsComp`, `homEquiv_extendScalarsComp`) is a **red herring**:
55	  those are needed for change-of-rings *associativity isos*, never touched here.
56	- **Project's current path**: docstring (L2127–2142) predicts "~150-LOC ModuleCat change-of-rings
57	  coherence (`restrictScalarsComp` / `homEquiv_extendScalarsComp`)". This over-estimates by ~3×.
58	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost (the project's own size/route estimate is wrong; following it would
59	  waste a prover dispatch building unnecessary `extendScalarsComp` machinery).
60	- **Verdict**: ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB (use `restrictScalars_μ_tmul`; drop the `extendScalarsComp` plan).
61	
62	### Decision 3 (directive Q3): Does `pushforwardComp = Iso.refl` reduce μ-equality to `μ_natural`/`map_comp`?
63	
64	- **Answer: NO.** `pushforwardComp = Iso.refl` is a statement about the **underlying functor**
65	  (`pushforward ψ ⋙ pushforward φ` and `pushforward χ` are defeq as functors). But the two μ's are two
66	  **different `LaxMonoidal` instances on that (defeq-)same functor**: the LHS instance is the
67	  Lean-synthesized `Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp (pushforward ψ) (pushforward φ)` (CONFIRMED in the goal),
68	  the RHS instance is `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal (φ ≫ F.op ◁ ψ)`. Their agreement is genuine
69	  content, NOT a `μ_natural` consequence. EMPIRICALLY CONFIRMED: `rfl` fails even on a single pure
70	  tensor `m ⊗ₜ n` (because `restrictScalars`'s μ is built by adjunction transpose
71	  `rightAdjointLaxMonoidal`, equal-but-not-defeq to the identity — it only *equals* `m⊗ₜn` via the
72	  proven `restrictScalars_μ_tmul`). The `d3sq2b258` "rfl/short ext" prediction is correctly refuted.
73	- **Verdict**: PROCEED (Q3 answered NO; not the route).
74	
75	## Recommendation
76	
77	Build it as a SHORT sectionwise proof via Mathlib's `ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul`, funnelled
78	through ONE project-local helper that unfolds the opaque `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` μ. Do NOT
79	build `extendScalarsComp` machinery. The helper is necessary because the `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal`
80	instance is `exact h`-cast (not syntactic `comp`), so neither `simp [comp_μ]` nor a direct
81	`erw [restrictScalars_μ_tmul]` fires on `μ (pushforward _)` — and the direct `erw` **whnf-explodes**
82	(>200000 heartbeats, even with `respectTransparency false`), exactly the catastrophic-whnf hazard the
83	project's memory repeatedly flags. See the concrete recipe in the report (`task_results/...`).
84	
85	## Empirical findings (iter-260, via `lean_multi_attempt` on the live goal)
86	
87	1. `ext W x; induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on` SPLITS cleanly (zero / tmul / add). ✓
88	2. LHS instance is synthesized as `Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp (pushforward ψ) (pushforward φ)`; `simp only
89	   [Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp_μ]` UNFOLDS it to
90	   `μ(pushforward φ) (Pψ X)(Pψ Y) ≫ (pushforward φ).map (μ(pushforward ψ) X Y)`. ✓
91	3. The inner `μ(pushforward φ)`, `μ(pushforward ψ)`, and RHS `μ(pushforward χ)` all carry the
92	   `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` instance and DO NOT unfold under `simp`/`comp_μ` (the `exact h`
93	   cast hides the `comp`). ← the central obstacle.
94	4. Direct `erw [ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul]` on these → **whnf timeout** (>200000), even with
95	   `set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in`. So a dedicated small-goal helper is
96	   mandatory; the explosion is from `erw` whnf-ing the heavy ambient term, not from the lemma itself.
97	5. `restrictScalarsLaxμ` (PresheafInternalHom.lean:306) defines the presheaf `restrictScalars` μ
98	   sectionwise (`app X := μ (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X).hom) (M₁.obj X) (M₂.obj X)`), and its
99	   `.naturality` (lines 313–318) already closes with `erw [..., ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul, ...]`
100	   under `respectTransparency false` — the proven in-file pattern the helper should mirror.

1107	/-- **The presheaf-of-modules pushforward is lax monoidal** (the analogist-named `μ_G`,
1108	Mathlib-absent at the pin). For a morphism `φ : S₀ ⋙ forget₂ ⟶ F.op ⋙ (R₀ ⋙ forget₂)`
1109	of presheaves of *commutative* rings, `PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ` unfolds to
1110	`pushforward₀OfCommRingCat F R₀ ⋙ restrictScalars φ`, the composite of the strong-monoidal
1111	topological pushforward `pushforward₀OfCommRingCat` (Mathlib) and the lax-monoidal
1112	`restrictScalars φ` (project `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`), hence lax monoidal by
1113	`Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp`.
1114	
1115	The hypothesis necessarily uses the *inner* `forget₂` association (`F.op ⋙ (R₀ ⋙ forget₂)`,
1116	the form `PresheafOfModules.pushforward` expects), but the monoidal-category instance on the
1117	middle presheaf is keyed on the *outer* association `(F.op ⋙ R₀) ⋙ forget₂` (the form
1118	`pushforward₀OfCommRingCat`'s target carries). The two are defeq; bridging them with a local
1119	`MonoidalCategory` instance triggers a kernel-rejected diamond, so instead `φ` is defeq-cast
1120	to the outer form (`φ'`) for the `restrictScalars` factor, and the resulting composite — defeq
1121	to `pushforward φ` — is transported back by `exact`. This is the right-adjoint lax structure
1122	that an eventual oplax comparison `δ` on the abstract left-adjoint pullback would inherit.
1123	Project-local supplement; reusable for the general pullback-monoidality build. -/

input.new_string:
/-- **Sectionwise value of the `pushforward` lax tensorator on a pure tensor.** The lax μ of a
single `PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ`, evaluated at a section `W` on a pure tensor `m ⊗ₜ n`, is
again the pure tensor `m ⊗ₜ n`. This unfolds the opaque `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` μ (which is
the `Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp` of `pushforward₀`'s μ = identity and `restrictScalars`'s μ) down to
`ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul` (the lax μ of `restrictScalars` is the identity on pure tensors).
Isolated as a small-goal helper so `restrictScalars_μ_tmul` fires without the catastrophic `whnf`
explosion that a direct `erw` on the composite-pushforward μ triggers. -/
private lemma pushforward_μ_app_tmul
    {C D : Type u} [Category.{u} C] [Category.{u} D] {F : C ⥤ D}
    {R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}} {S₀ : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
    (φ : (S₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
      F.op ⋙ (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
    (A B : _root_.PresheafOfModules (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
    (W : Cᵒᵖ)
    (m : ↑(((PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ).obj A).obj W))
    (n : ↑(((PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ).obj B).obj W)) :
    (ModuleCat.Hom.hom ((Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ) A B).app W))
      (m ⊗ₜ[↑((S₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat).obj W)] n) = m ⊗ₜ n := by
  sorry

/-- **Sectionwise reduction of the `pushforward` lax tensorator to the `restrictScalars` μ.** The lax
μ of a single `PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ`, evaluated at a section `W`, equals the `ModuleCat`
lax μ of `restrictScalars (φ.app W).hom`. This unfolds the opaque `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` μ

carrier-duality (`S₀ W` vs `R₀ (F.op W)` module structures on the same carriers) never surfaces.
Isolated as a small-goal helper so the reduction fires without the catastrophic `whnf` explosion that
a direct `erw [restrictScalars_μ_tmul]` on the composite-pushforward μ triggers. -/
private lemma pushforward_μ_app_eq
    {C D : Type u} [Category.{u} C] [Category.{u} D] {F : C ⥤ D}
    {R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}} {S₀ : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
    (φ : (S₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶

input.new_string:
/-- **Reduction of the `pushforward` lax tensorator to the `restrictScalars` μ (morphism level).**
The lax μ of a single `PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ` equals the lax μ of the change-of-rings
`restrictScalars φ'` on the (strongly-monoidal, `μIso = refl`) reindexed objects
`pushforward₀OfCommRingCat F R₀`. This unfolds the opaque `presheafPushforwardLaxMonoidal` μ (the
`Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp` of `pushforward₀`'s μ = identity and `restrictScalars`'s μ) to the
directly-computable `restrictScalars` μ — staying at the `PresheafOfModules` morphism level so the
`(presheaf-tensor).obj W` vs `ModuleCat`-tensor mismatch never surfaces. Mirrors the ε-twin
`epsilonPresheafToSheafUnit`. -/
private lemma pushforward_μ_eq
    {C D : Type u} [Category.{u} C] [Category.{u} D] {F : C ⥤ D}
    {R₀ : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}} {S₀ : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
    (φ : (S₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
      F.op ⋙ (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
    (A B : _root_.PresheafOfModules (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) :
    letI φ' : (S₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
        (F.op ⋙ R₀) ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat := φ
    Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ) A B
      = Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ')
          ((PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀OfCommRingCat F R₀).obj A)
          ((PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀OfCommRingCat F R₀).obj B) := by
  sorry

`PresheafOfModules.comp_app` distributes the composite. Direct `restrictScalars_μ_tmul` still whnf-explodes on the heavy ambient. I need a `rfl`-helper to rewrite `(μ (restrictScalars α) M₁ M₂).app W` to the `ModuleCat` μ first (so `restrictScalars_μ_tmul` matches syntactically). Let me add it.

input.old_string: /-- **Reduction of the `pushforward` lax tensorator to the `restrictScalars` μ (morphism level).**
input.new_string:
/-- **Sectionwise value of the presheaf `restrictScalars` lax tensorator.** The lax μ of
`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars α`, evaluated at a section `W`, is by definition the `ModuleCat`
lax μ of `restrictScalars (α.app W).hom`. Exposed as a `rfl`-lemma so the heavy ambient term need not
be `whnf`-ed: rewriting with it turns `(μ (restrictScalars α) M₁ M₂).app W` into a `ModuleCat` μ on
which `ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul` matches syntactically (a direct `erw` on the presheaf form
`whnf`-explodes). -/
private lemma restrictScalars_μ_app
    {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {R S : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
    (α : (R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶ (S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
    (M₁ M₂ : _root_.PresheafOfModules (S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) (W : Cᵒᵖ) :
    (Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars α) M₁ M₂).app W
      = Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app W).hom)
          (M₁.obj W) (M₂.obj W) := rfl

  -- KEY REDUCTION (iter-260, established): `pushforward_μ_eq` rewrites every `μ (pushforward _)` to
  -- the lighter `μ (restrictScalars _)` on the strongly-monoidal `pushforward₀` objects, and
  -- `Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp_μ` unfolds the composite-pushforward μ.  On a pure tensor every
  -- `restrictScalars` μ is the identity (`ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul`), so both sides collapse
  -- to `m ⊗ₜ n`.  Remaining mechanical work: tensor-induction zero/add closers, HO-matching of
  -- `restrictScalars_μ_app` (forces the `restrictScalars` μ `.app W` into `ModuleCat` μ so
  -- `restrictScalars_μ_tmul` fires without the `whnf` blow-up of a direct `erw`), and the
  -- `(pushforward φ).map (μ (restrictScalars ψ) …)` reindex leg (`.app W = … .app (F.op.obj W)`).
  induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on with
  | zero => sorry
  | add a b ha hb => sorry
  | tmul m n =>
    simp only [pushforward_μ_eq, Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp_μ]
    rw [PresheafOfModules.comp_app, ModuleCat.comp_apply]
    sorry

-- KEY REDUCTION (iter-260, established): `pushforward_μ_eq` rewrites every `μ (pushforward _)` to
  -- the lighter `μ (restrictScalars _)` on the strongly-monoidal `pushforward₀` objects, and
  -- `Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp_μ` unfolds the composite-pushforward μ.  On a pure tensor every
  -- `restrictScalars` μ is the identity (`ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul`), so both sides collapse
  -- to `m ⊗ₜ n`.
  refine PresheafOfModules.hom_ext (fun W => ?_)
  refine ModuleCat.MonoidalCategory.tensor_ext (fun m n => ?_)
  simp only [pushforward_μ_eq, Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp_μ]
  rw [PresheafOfModules.comp_app, ModuleCat.comp_apply]
  sorry
